# Reviewable Architecture Studies {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box, a British statistician who made major contributions to time-series analysis, famously noted that while all models are flawed, the good ones are practically useful. For architecture work, fast proxies and surrogate models are useful only when the record states their scope, calibration, and failure modes. They can accelerate scoped exploration, but they do not by themselves justify commitment.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter formalizes how a bounded AI-assisted architecture study becomes reviewable. It distinguishes the study, its artifacts and architectural claim, the design loop used to execute and analyze the work, and the compact records that let another architect inspect the result.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must an AI-assisted architecture study record before another architect can
evaluate, challenge, and defend its claims?
:::

Computer architecture has always depended on disciplined abstraction. An
architect rarely reasons directly from every transistor to every application
behavior. The field instead builds models, simulators, workload
characterizations, cost estimates, design rules, and review practices that make
large design spaces tractable. That quantitative tradition is central to
modern architecture practice [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].
It also explains why Architecture 2.0 should not be framed as a sudden break
from the past. The continuity lies in disciplined abstraction, measurement,
tools, experiments, and judgment. The shift is that AI can now participate
across formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and
defense, including parts of the process that produces the architectural claim.
Wider participation changes the failure mode. A plausible error can propagate
through tools and decisions faster when scope, counterevidence, and authority
remain implicit.

An artifact is meaningful only in the context of the study that produced it.
Authors should document the question, alternatives, tools, experiments,
interpretation, and resulting decision. For iterative work, they should also
record the relevant design state, allowed changes, tool results, revisions, and
decision authority so another architect can reconstruct how the conclusion was reached
[@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. The five-part execution view of
@sec-moonshot (what a system can see, what it can change, how feedback is
obtained, what evidence is trusted, and what can reject the result) is one
reading of those obligations, viewing the same loop that @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
diagnosed from the angle of what a study must record. Without that structure, an AI system may still produce plausible
text, code, or configurations, but its output cannot support a consequential
architecture claim on plausibility alone.

@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales identified five ways an architecture process can become
constrained. This chapter specifies what a reviewer needs to examine in each
case, as summarized in @tbl-bounds-to-framework. The architectural claim is the
unit of review, and a compact card introduced later indexes the supporting
records.

| **Bound** | **Framework element that answers it** |
| --- | --- |
| Representation-bound | Representations and scoped consequence models state what study participants can see, predict, and constrain. |
| Action-bound | The legal design space and tool environments define which actions are valid. |
| Feedback-bound | The evidence record and its fidelity rules say when feedback may support a claim. |
| Rejection-bound | Declared rejection conditions, checks, and authority say what can block advancement. |
| Commitment-bound | The evidence-supported claim boundary limits what may be claimed, and the named decision owner limits what the study may commit to. |

: **Records needed for the five bottlenecks.** Each row identifies the information a reviewer needs when representation, available actions, feedback, rejection capacity, or commitment limits the study. {#tbl-bounds-to-framework tbl-colwidths="[24,76]"}

Other fields do not supply a ready-made Architecture 2.0 standard. They do show
which properties must be recorded when claims, workflows, and consequences grow
too complex for one person to remember. Popper's falsifiability criterion
supplies one useful starting point. A claim should state what observation could
count against it, not only what evidence would make it look stronger.[^fn-popper-falsifiability]

[^fn-popper-falsifiability]: Popper developed the criterion in *Logik der
Forschung*, published in German in 1934 and later in English as *The Logic of
Scientific Discovery* [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. Here
falsifiability belongs to an architectural claim and its test protocol, not to
an AI model as an intrinsic property. It asks what observable result would
conflict with the claim. It does not imply that one failed run establishes the
root cause or ends the inquiry.

Not every architecture decision is a universal scientific hypothesis. The
transferable requirement is more modest and more useful. State the scope, name
the observation that would count against the claim, and keep the decision open
to revision when stronger evidence arrives. Several established fields already
work this way, each documenting a different part of that practice.
@tbl-cross-field-method-lineage compares the external practice, what it records,
what transfers to Architecture 2.0, and where the analogy stops.

| **External practice** | **What it records** | **What transfers to Architecture 2.0** | **Where the analogy stops** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Experimental science | A claim, test conditions, observations that could conflict with it, and provisional rather than final acceptance [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. | State the claim and non-claim, predeclare counterevidence, and preserve results that weaken the preferred explanation. | Tool failure, model mismatch, and scope error can also explain a failed run, so rejection still requires diagnosis. |
| Experimental science: high-energy physics | Data, code, computational environment, workflow steps, provenance, versions, and internal review state captured during the analysis [@ChenEtAl2016CERNAnalysisPreservation]. | Preserve versioned design trajectories and runnable inputs while the work is happening, not as a reconstruction after publication. | A preserved workflow can support replay without establishing that its claim, baseline, or decision was adequate. |
| Experimental science: translational bioinformatics failure analysis | Raw data, transformations, labels, and code needed to reconstruct a predictor after undocumented processing hid simple errors; in the cited case, trial allocation was already occurring [@BaggerlyCoombes2009]. | Record transformations and establish credible challenge before a claim justifies consequential commitment. | The cited case exposed failure after clinical use began; it does not show that pre-use independent reanalysis was already standard practice. |
| Systems safety and assurance | System constraints, process assumptions, control and feedback paths, evidence arguments, responsibility, and waivers [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld; @Kelly2004GSN]. | Review the whole sociotechnical loop rather than treating a capable model or passing component check as sufficient. | Most architecture exploration is not safety certification; evidence and independence should scale with consequence. |
| Site-reliability engineering | Observability, error budgets, runbooks, rollback, and revalidation under drift [@BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. | Expand automated authority where actions are monitored and reversible, and narrow it as rollback becomes costly. | Silicon, interfaces, and long-lived hardware commitments are not as cheaply reversible as software deployments. |
| Scientific databases and benchmarks | Shared data, metadata, interfaces, workloads, and reporting rules that let results travel across groups [@JainEtAl2013MaterialsProject; @SPEC2017CPU; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. | Build common datasets and comparison protocols around stated task and evidence scope. | A dataset or leaderboard alone does not record why a design was rejected or who authorized the resulting claim. |

: **Other fields record claims, workflows, evidence, challenges, and decisions:** Architecture 2.0 borrows these properties without importing another field's institutions or vocabulary unchanged. {#tbl-cross-field-method-lineage tbl-colwidths="[18,27,29,18]"}

The first three rows draw on experimental science, including examples from
high-energy physics and bioinformatics. The next two draw on systems safety and
site-reliability engineering, while the final row draws on scientific
databases and benchmarking. EDA is omitted because it is part of computer
architecture's own practice rather than an external influence. These examples
also show why retained logs are insufficient. Logs preserve observations and
provenance, but reviewers still need the question, alternatives,
counterevidence, mechanism, challenge process, and decision authority.

The framework is meant for four ordinary tasks. Researchers can describe the
structure of an Architecture 2.0 contribution, reviewers can reconstruct a
study and find the evidence for its claim, tool builders can specify a harness
or environment, and authors can state what AI contributed to a design problem.
If it does not help with those tasks, it is only vocabulary.

Architecture lacks a widely
adopted cross-tool layer that connects formulation, experiments, evidence, and
decisions above RTL. From RTL down, design flows already have artifacts that
tools can parse, transform, reject, and sign off. Although high-level synthesis (HLS)
flows, modeling languages, compiler IRs, and EDA frameworks structure parts of
the path to implementation, much of the reasoning that decides *what* should be
built still lives in whiteboards, spreadsheets, scripts, slides, review
memories, and natural-language specifications. The design-loop card gives a
reviewer one compact index into that work. It can point to the study's tool
contract (@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces) and detailed
evidence record rather than copying either.

Compiler infrastructure gives a useful but limited analogy. MLIR
(@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales) exposed many compiler transformations to tools and reviewers
[@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. Architecture 2.0 is not asking for one universal
architecture IR. It is asking for a claim-and-evidence record where
design intent, workload scope, feedback, and challenge can be checked before an
artifact is trusted.

The hierarchy in this chapter is simple. A bounded architecture study is the
unit of work. Its intended outcome is better architectural understanding,
artifacts, findings, or decisions. An artifact is an implementation or proposal
the study produces, and the architectural claim is the unit another architect
reviews. A study may use a fixed execution and analysis process; when the
work iterates, that process is a design loop. The
design-loop card is a compact index into the study, claim, artifacts, evidence,
and decision.

That layer sits between tacit reasoning and implementation flows
(@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl). It records the architecture question,
comparison, tool path, evidence, and pending decision in a form that tools,
automated participants, reviewers, and architects can inspect together.

![**A reviewable architecture study connects intent to implementation evidence:** The bounded study contains the scope, architectural claim, execution and analysis process, and evidence needed for review. Iterative work uses a design loop. A compact card indexes these records without replacing them.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="100%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing tacit architecture reasoning flowing into a reviewable architecture study, which connects to executable tools and returns implementation evidence for review."}

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can construct a bounded AI-assisted architecture study
that another architect can review. That means you can:

- **Bound:** State the study's workload, baseline, design space, constraints, non-goals, and intended decision.
- **Challenge:** State an architectural claim together with supporting evidence and observations that would weaken or overturn it.
- **Distinguish:** Separate the study, its artifacts, its claim, its execution loop, and its supporting records.
- **Map:** Connect method roles to represented state, legal actions, tools, feedback, evidence, and assigned authority.
- **Use:** Read the ontology and design-loop card diagnostically without treating completion of either as proof of architectural quality.
:::

## The Bounded Study Is the Unit of Work

> **Bounded architecture study.** A bounded architecture study is a scoped unit
> of architecture work that names its question, workload or scenario, baseline,
> legal design space, objectives, constraints, non-goals, representations and
> tools, planned evidence, and intended decision.

A study may produce better architectural understanding, an implementation, a
design-space map, a finding, or a decision. Those outcomes need not all take the
same form, but another architect should be able to recover the comparison that
made them meaningful and the evidence that limits what may be concluded. The
study is therefore broader than the artifact it produces and broader than the
design loop used to carry out the work.

## The Architectural Claim Is the Unit of Review

Asking whether a generative model[^fn-ml-generative-model] can design hardware is too coarse a framing. The more
architecture-native approach asks what specific claim is being made and what evidence would make it credible. Architects rarely accept an artifact by itself. They
accept or reject a claim about that artifact relative to a workload, baseline,
design space, objectives, constraints, and evidence. The definition below names
that review object so the rest of the chapter can ask what an AI-assisted result
actually establishes.

[^fn-ml-generative-model]: A class of AI models designed to generate new data that resembles its training data, such as text or configurations. In an architecture design loop, such a model proposes candidate artifacts inside the legal design space. Its output still must be tested as a claim rather than trusted as a result.

> **Architectural claim.** An architectural claim states that an artifact,
> mechanism, relationship, finding, or decision improves, preserves, explains,
> or rules out specified hardware/software behavior for a workload or scenario
> relative to a baseline. It also states the legal design space, objectives,
> constraints, claim boundary (the evidence-supported scope of what is and is
> not claimed), supporting evidence, and observations that would
> weaken or overturn it.

> **Rejection authority, conditions, and checks.** A rejection condition names
> an observable result
> that counts against a candidate or claim. A rejection check is the test, tool,
> rule, or review that evaluates that condition. Rejection authority is the
> declared power to block, weaken, or redirect advancement, which may be
> automated under a stated policy. The decision owner remains
> responsible for consequential commitment, exceptions, and waivers.

> **Commitment boundary.** The commitment boundary is the strongest next
> decision the current evidence permits.

Across these definitions, one separation is deliberate. The strongest supported
claim and the authority to act on it remain separate; evidence bounds what may
be claimed, while a declared owner decides what the organization does about it.

The phrase "for a workload or scenario" matters because evidence from one
operating context may not apply in another.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Valid for the last rocket, not this one"}
The Ariane 5 rocket reused inertial-reference software that had flown on Ariane 4. Those flights supplied operational evidence inside the Ariane 4 envelope, not a correctness guarantee for a new vehicle. On Ariane 5's different, higher-velocity trajectory, a value overflowed a conversion the older profile never reached, both channels shut down, and the vehicle self-destructed about forty seconds after launch. The inquiry traced the loss to a software specification and design error together with tests that did not represent Ariane 5 flight conditions [@Lions1996Ariane5]. The prior evidence did not cover the scenario to which the component was now committed.

An architectural claim is valid only for the workload and operating conditions
covered by its evidence. An AI-assisted study that reuses a trusted component
must check the evidence again for the new workload because the claim, not the
reused code, must still hold.
:::

@tbl-architectural-claim-schema organizes the information needed to review a
claim into five parts and illustrates each part with the executed study.

| **Part of the claim record** | **Reader question** | **Executed-study instance** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Claim and non-claims** | What exact result is asserted, and what remains outside it? | Separate records ask whether a nonbaseline geometry wins, whether the model arm beats the conventional arm, and whether the proposed mechanism predicts the probe. Energy, area, RTL, physical design, and product fitness remain non-claims. |
| **Comparison boundary** | For which workload, baseline, and legal design space? | One frozen three-layer GEMM workload, a 32 by 32 baseline, and legal row-by-column geometries under 1,024 processing elements. |
| **Objective and constraints** | What counts as improvement, and what cannot be violated? | A preregistered cycle-and-utilization score, a 1 percent improvement margin, legal geometry, and a cycle deadline. |
| **Evidence and challenge** | What supports or weakens each claim? | Equal SCALE-Sim budgets, every candidate result, replay bindings, and a matched transposition probe. |
| **Decision and authority** | What action does the evidence support, and who may authorize it? | The evidence recommends retaining the baseline and stopping; it does not authorize RTL or product action, and the named owner must confirm the decision separately. |

: **Information needed to review an architectural claim.** Each result is reported with its scope, comparison, evidence, possible counterevidence, and authorized decision. In the executed study, the architecture outcome, AI contribution, and mechanism could fail separately, so the tie and falsified explanation remained informative. {#tbl-architectural-claim-schema tbl-colwidths="[21,31,38]"}

```{=latex}
\Needspace{18\baselineskip}
```

The AlphaChip debate shows why those fields must describe one comparison rather
than several partially overlapping ones.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The public record did not define one common comparison"}
**The claim.** The AlphaChip paper stated a concrete macro-placement task and reported results
at expert quality [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** Independent researchers later released test cases and evaluation flows, but they could not reconstruct
all of the proprietary designs, training context, and implementation details
behind the strongest reported results. The original authors, in turn, argued
that the reassessment had not run the method as described
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** Those gaps do not show by themselves that the reported result was wrong. They
show why an outside reviewer could not recover one comparison accepted by both
sides. A reviewable claim needs a stated baseline, scope, tool path, evidence,
and decision boundary before disagreement begins; otherwise later reviewers
must infer the study from an incomplete public record.
:::

Stating the claim this way also clarifies what AI systems are being asked to do. Generation
can propose artifacts inside the legal design space. Prediction can estimate
objectives before expensive feedback. Optimization can search tradeoffs under
constraints. Critique and verification can apply declared rejection checks.
The architectural result may be understanding, an artifact, a finding, or a
decision. Another architect can review the result only when the study states
the comparison, scope, and evidence on which it depends.

::: {#pri-state-the-claim .callout-design-principle title="State the claim before evaluating it"}
Before evaluating an AI-assisted result, state what it claims, the workload and
baseline, the legal design space, the objective and constraints, the evidence
that could support or overturn it, and the person responsible for the decision.
Without those terms, the output may be interesting, but the architectural claim
is not yet testable.
:::

## The Design Loop Is an Execution and Analysis Process

Once the claim is stated, the architect must define the study's execution and
analysis process. When the work iterates across AI methods and tools, that
process is a design loop. Such work goes beyond a single act of generation. It
frames a problem, chooses abstractions, explores
alternatives, measures candidates, rejects weak results, revises assumptions,
and decides when evidence is strong enough to commit.

> **Architecture design loop.** An architecture design loop is the repeated
> work of inspecting the current design state, making an allowed change, running
> the relevant tools, evaluating the result, and deciding whether to revise,
> reject, or continue. The study records enough of each iteration for another
> architect to understand why the next step was taken.

> **Study execution specification.** A study execution specification lists the
> state a design loop may read and write, its permitted actions, the feedback
> and evidence rules applied to observations, and the rejection and decision
> authority governing its results.

For Architecture 2.0, the design loop is one process through which an AI
system participates. If the execution specification omits state, actions,
feedback, or stopping rules, the system is only producing outputs, not
participating credibly in the study.
The discussions from @sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-feedback-verification-trust develop these fields. The environment contract
in @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces is the tool-facing subset of
the execution specification.

For the lighthouse prompt, the distinction is immediate. A request for a
low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for XRBench-class mobile XR
under a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope sounds compact. But the
prompt does not yet define a bounded study. It does not say which workload traces
are authoritative, which vector operations matter, which memory hierarchy is
admissible, which software stack must run, which simulator is trusted, which
power model applies, which process assumptions are available, which
alternatives must be considered, or what evidence is enough to reject a
candidate. These choices define the architecture problem and must precede a
generative method's response.

During one iteration, a participant reads the recorded design state, makes an
allowed change, and runs a tool. The team then decides whether the result counts
as evidence and whether to accept, reject, revise, or escalate the candidate.
Objectives and constraints bound the work; stopping and escalation rules
determine whether another iteration is justified. The following
equation is only a compact explanation of that update:
$$
s_{t+1} =
\operatorname{Update}(s_t, a_t, o_t, e_t, d_t).
$$
Here, $s_t$ is the represented architecture state, $a_t$ is the permitted
action taken by the loop, $o_t$ is the observation returned by the
environment, and $e_t$ records evidence only when the study identifies the
relevant claim and scope, explains how the observation should be interpreted,
and records its provenance;
otherwise it is empty. The term
$d_t$ is the human or policy decision to accept, reject,
revise, or escalate. This equation does not claim that every architecture
loop is a Markov decision process[^fn-rl-mdp]. It is a bookkeeping view of the
same turn summarized by the design-loop card. If a loop
cannot say how actions, feedback, evidence, and decisions update state, then
it is not yet represented well enough for credible AI-mediated architecture
work (@fig-design-loop).

[^fn-rl-mdp]: A mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision maker; it forms the foundation of reinforcement learning. The chapter borrows the loop's notation from this framework while cautioning that an architecture loop need not literally be one.

![**How an iterative architecture study proceeds.** A participant reads the recorded design state, makes an allowed change, runs a tool, and submits the result for review. The reviewer may narrow or accept the claim, reject the candidate, or request another iteration. Fidelity, commitment, and allowed authority remain separate choices.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="100%" fig-alt="A design loop within a scoped architecture study. Represented state, permitted action, tool feedback, and evidence lead to claim review. A scoped finding or decision can leave the loop, while revision updates the study state for another turn."}

Study and claim remain the units of work and review; the design loop is what we
examine when we ask how the work was carried out, because a model is only one
participant in it. A method may generate candidates, summarize
evidence, predict outcomes, call tools, critique assumptions, or coordinate
subtasks. Its output becomes credible through the study's comparisons,
experiments, and review, not through the model in isolation.

A microarchitectural choice can affect several layers of the system. It may
require compiler support above the hardware interface or create timing and
routing problems below it. No participant, human or automated, should be
presumed to know the complete state of every layer. The study must therefore
record the assumptions and consequences that matter at each affected layer.

## Design Spaces Make Claims Meaningful

An architectural claim is meaningful only relative to a design space. A system
that reports "the best" candidate without exposing the alternatives, invalid
regions, baseline, and tradeoffs has not made an architecture result easy to
review. It has hidden the comparison that gives the result meaning.

In architecture, the design space is not the set of all strings a model might
emit. It is a constrained set of architecturally legal choices:
$$
\mathcal{D} = \{x \in X \mid x \text{ is valid under the task and architecture
constraints}\}.
$$
Here, $X$ is that unconstrained space of candidate artifacts, everything a
method could emit. The validity conditions may include ISA compatibility, memory semantics,
software support, timing assumptions, power limits, package constraints,
verification requirements, and deployment policy. The current representation
and toolchain usually support only an executable subset of $\mathcal{D}$. A
candidate inside the legal space but outside that subset reveals an environment
or representation gap; it should be deferred or evaluated through another path,
not mislabeled as architecturally invalid. A candidate outside $\mathcal{D}$ is
invalid for the stated study unless the study revises its scope or
constraints and records why.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. A 64-bit RISC-V-based mobile XR
subsystem might vary vector width, cache and memory hierarchy, accelerator
partitioning, dataflow, clocking, voltage assumptions, compiler/runtime
support, and verification scope. Some choices are legal but unattractive. Some
are attractive under a proxy but fail at higher fidelity. Some violate the
power envelope, process assumptions, software contract, or workload coverage.
An Architecture 2.0 study must represent those distinctions. Otherwise its
methods cannot distinguish improvement from exploitation of a hole in the
problem statement.

Most architecture studies must evaluate several objectives at once, including
performance, energy, latency, area, reliability, programmability, verification
effort, and cost. A design-space report should therefore document the
alternatives explored, the tradeoffs observed, the candidates rejected, and the
decisions that remain.

## The Architecture 2.0 Ontology {#sec-arch2-ontology}

A method label is not enough to review an architecture claim. A paper can say
it uses an LLM, reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization], or a surrogate
model and still leave the important relationships invisible. The reader does
not yet know how the task was scoped, what actions were legal, what feedback was
used, what failed, or who accepted the commitment. A taxonomy can group methods,
benchmarks, tools, or tasks, but those categories will change with the field.

[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization]: A strategy for optimizing expensive black-box functions, where each candidate evaluation (such as a cycle-accurate simulation) is costly. The chapter names it as one method a paper might cite, and stresses that the label matters less than the represented study state, available evaluation resources, and challenge rules the method operates under.

> **Architecture 2.0 ontology.** The Architecture 2.0 ontology is a conceptual
> relationship map. It names the entities and relationships that make a bounded
> AI-assisted architecture study representable, executable, open to review, and
> owned; it is not a formal machine-interpretable ontology.

As a preview of @sec-data-representations-world-models, an architecture world
model is an action-conditioned predictor of how represented state changes after
a proposed action [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels;
@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. It may live in a simulator, surrogate,
or calibrated cost model. The broader term *consequence model* also includes
rules and constraint checkers that bound actions without predicting what
follows. @sec-data-representations-world-models gives the canonical treatment.

The ontology records how the parts of a study depend on one another. The stated
intent defines the task, which determines what information participants need
and which changes they may make. Tools define valid actions and return
measurements. The team selects methods that fit those actions and the available
evaluation budget, then decides which results count as evidence and whether to
accept, reject, revise, or escalate the work. The design-loop card provides a
compact index to these records. The state update, reading questions, and card
describe the same study rather than separate forms to complete.

The supporting evidence record captures what the study actually observed and
how that observation affected the claim. @sec-feedback-verification-trust
examines feedback and evidence in detail.

This is why ontology should precede taxonomy. Before classifying whether a paper
uses an LLM[^fn-ml-llm], reinforcement learning[^fn-rl-method-label], Bayesian
optimization, a surrogate, or a simulator wrapper, identify the study
and its claim. Once the task, actions, feedback, evidence, challenge, and decision relationships
are stated, method categories become useful. Before that, labels can hide more
than they reveal.

[^fn-ml-llm]: Large Language Model, a neural network trained on vast amounts of text to understand and generate human-like language. As a method label, the term reveals little until the study, role, and represented state are identified.

[^fn-rl-method-label]: For Architecture 2.0, the label "reinforcement learning" still does not say what state the agent saw, which actions were legal, which reward proxied the objective, or which rejection conditions bounded the loop.

The practical implication is not to build a chip-specific language model[^fn-ml-language-model] first.
AI methods can contribute only when practitioners describe the relevant
artifacts, allowed changes, tool results, review criteria, and decision limits
clearly enough for the methods to act and for experts to assess the results.
The framework therefore starts with the structure of architecture work rather
than a catalog of models.

[^fn-ml-language-model]: A statistical model trained to predict the next word or token in a sequence, forming the foundation of modern AI text generation. Building a domain-specific model is not a precondition for AI-addressable architecture; representing the study's objects, actions, and evidence is.

## The Compact Framework

@fig-ontology-chain summarizes the framework. The study defines the question
and alternatives. The architectural claim states what reviewers must evaluate.
Representations, tools, and method assignments determine what participants can
do, and the evidence limits what they may conclude.

The architectural claim, not the model output, is the review object. For
practical work, the design-loop card developed below provides one compact
index. The state update, figures, and grouped questions explain relationships
inside that card.

Reviewers first identify the question, baseline, and legal design space. They
then examine what participants could observe and change, which tools and methods
were used, and what evaluation limits applied. Finally, they assess the
evidence, rejected alternatives, explanation, decision authority, and scope of
the resulting claim. These are review concerns rather than fixed execution
stages.

These reading passes are intentionally stricter than a demonstration needs to
be. A system can be useful while still leaving its action bounds,
evidence standard, rejection checks, decision rights, or commitment
underspecified.

The card is a compact index to information already required by the framework.
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives it a
machine-checkable schema with independent context, inspectability, replay,
independent-review, disclosure, and decision-right profiles. A validator can
confirm that required fields, internal links, scoped rights, and local integrity
bindings are present. One profile does not imply another, and structural
completion does not establish evidence quality.

@tbl-compact-framework-checklist applies these review questions to the
lighthouse study.

![**The framework connects a scoped study to an architectural decision:** The study establishes scope, the claim defines what must be reviewed, and representations and environments make action possible. When work iterates, a design loop organizes execution and analysis. Evidence supports review by the authorized owner, who may advance a finding, narrow the claim, or revise the study.](images/F4-ontology-chain){#fig-ontology-chain width="100%" fig-alt="A framework connecting a scoped architecture study and its architectural claim to representations, tools, an execution and analysis process, evidence, review by an authorized owner, and a scoped finding or decision."}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

| **Reading pass** | **Reader question** | **Lighthouse instance** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Scope** | What claim is being made, for which workload and baseline, inside which legal design space? | Improve mobile XR efficiency within a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope while recording the workload, baseline, legal RISC-V choices, and deferred work. |
| **Execution** | What state can the loop read and change, what job does each method perform, and what feedback can it afford? | Workload traces, architecture parameters, compiler assumptions, a simulator or cost model, constrained candidate generation or search, and a declared evaluation budget. |
| **Judgment** | What supports or challenges the claim, what mechanism explains the result, and what commitment follows? | Pareto and sensitivity evidence, rejected candidates, an explanation grounded in utilization and memory traffic, declared rejection checks, and a scoped architectural decision. |

: **The framework can be read in three passes:** Scope, execution, and judgment each pair the reader's question with the corresponding lighthouse instance. {#tbl-compact-framework-checklist tbl-colwidths="[20,36,34]"}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

@tbl-architecture-translation-matrix translates common AI terms into the
specific architecture artifacts, actions, measurements, and checks they refer
to. Words such as state, action, observation, environment, reward, and critic
are useful only in that concrete context.
If a paper says an automated method acts in an environment, the reader should be able to
name the architecture state it reads, the action it is allowed to take, the
tool feedback it observes, the result that would count against the claim, and
the declared check and decision process.

| **Generic term** | **Architecture translation** | **Example artifacts or observations** | **Failure or challenge condition** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **State** | Workload, design, software, tool, constraint, and evidence state. | Traces, configs, RTL, compiler IR, simulator stats, EDA reports, review notes. | Missing provenance or hidden assumptions. |
| **Action** | Legal architecture, compiler, runtime, or tool-flow change. | Change cache size, vector width, mapping, schedule, constraint, partition, or test. | Invalid parameter, noncompilable code, nonsynthesizable RTL, or policy violation. |
| **Observation** | Feedback returned by a tool, benchmark, review, or deployment path. | Latency, energy, area, timing, congestion, warnings, failures, telemetry. | Wrong workload, stale tool version, simulator mismatch, or weak fidelity. |
| **Environment** | Tool-connected harness that defines legal actions and feedback. | Simulator wrapper, compiler pipeline, RTL flow, EDA stage, benchmark harness. | Unmodeled constraints, nondeterminism, incomplete logging, or invalid actions. |
| **Objective** | Stated architecture tradeoff, not a generic reward. | Performance, power, and area; tail latency; power envelope; reliability; carbon; cost; evidence budget. | Proxy gaming, lost Pareto tradeoff, or missing decision rule. |
| **Critic/verifier** | Declared challenge check that can weaken or reject a claim; independence is required when the consequence warrants it. | Tests, formal checks, baseline replay, cross-simulator comparison, signoff review. | Unsupported claim, failed check, counterexample, or insufficient evidence. |

: **AI terms need architecture translations:** Architecture 2.0 uses generic vocabulary only when each term is grounded in concrete hardware/software design objects, tool outputs, and challenge paths. {#tbl-architecture-translation-matrix tbl-colwidths="[16,28,28,18]"}

These relationships do not form a pipeline that runs once. A failed
simulation may revise the representation. A weak benchmark result may revise
the task. A provenance problem may invalidate the evidence. A rejection may
force an environment change as well as discard a candidate. Architecture 2.0 is
therefore not only about adding AI into existing work. It makes that
participation scoped, observable, and tied to assigned owners within an architecture
study.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Study Revision Check"}
When an AI method fails or produces an invalid result, the architect must decide whether the failure rejects only the candidate or requires revising the environment, design space, or evidence standard.
:::

## Authority Is Scoped, Not Inherent

Autonomous or non-autonomous is too coarse a description. Authority comes from
a scoped assignment; it is not implied by a model's capability. A study therefore
records separate decision rights rather than placing the work on one automation
ladder (@tbl-decision-rights-matrix).

| **Right** | **What it authorizes** | **Required record** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Propose** | Produce a candidate, hypothesis, test, or change for review. | Actor, allowed output type, scope, budget, and required provenance. |
| **Execute** | Run a declared action inside the environment. | Legal actions, resource limits, stop conditions, failure handling, and rollback path. |
| **Reject** | Block a candidate, claim, or transition when a named rejection check fails. | Check, criterion, evidence source, independence relationship, and rejector. |
| **Waive** | Permit a failed or unmet rejection check to be bypassed under stated conditions. | Waiver holder, rationale, expiration, residual risk, and reopening condition. |
| **Recommend** | Synthesize the evidence into a scope-limited next-action recommendation. | Recommender, supported scope, alternatives, non-claims, and uncertainty. |
| **Commit** | Authorize the consequential transition and accept its residual risk. | Rights holder, consequence, accepted evidence, and conditions for reversal or revalidation. |

: **Decision rights are independent assignments.** A participant may hold one
right without holding the others. For each right, the study should state its
scope, required evidence, and consequences. A model's technical capability does
not by itself grant authority. {#tbl-decision-rights-matrix tbl-colwidths="[14,34,42]"}

The separation matters in ordinary workflows. A model may propose a cache
configuration while a harness executes it, a simulator threshold rejects it, a
review lead controls waivers, an architect recommends a tradeoff, and a product
organization holds the commit right. One actor may hold several rights, but the
record must say so. No right silently inherits another.

Framing and commit rights remain architecture responsibilities
(@sec-what-architect-owns). That boundary does not limit what a method can do;
it identifies who must answer for the claim, comparison, evidence, and
consequences. A card can validate the assignments, but validation does not prove
that the chosen rejector is independent enough or that the evidence is strong
enough.

The appropriate scope depends on architecture-specific risk. A compiler flag
that can be rolled back after telemetry is not the same as an RTL change that
affects timing closure. A simulator configuration is not the same as a
mask-level choice. A benchmark-generation loop is not the same as a signoff
loop. The more irreversible the action, the stronger the evidence, independent
challenge, and named decision owner must be.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint}
Before assigning execute, reject, waive, recommend, or commit rights, locate the
allowed actions, evidence requirement, escalation path, decision owner, and
consequences. If any is missing, the assignment is not yet justified.
:::

The following sections preview conditions developed in
@sec-data-representations-world-models,
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces,
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, and
@sec-feedback-verification-trust. The chapter then returns to the design-loop
card.

## Intent Defines the Task

Product goals such as "improve mobile XR efficiency" are too broad to execute
directly. The architect must define a specific task, such as characterizing a
workload, comparing compute organizations, estimating memory traffic, or
preparing a design-space report. That choice determines what is in scope, what
can be measured, what cost of being wrong is acceptable, and how much authority
an AI method may exercise. Critiquing a report,
predicting energy, searching an accelerator tiling space, and editing RTL need
different state, feedback, and rejection conditions. "Use AI" is therefore
never the task. The study must bound the work before a method is chosen; a card
can index that boundary for review.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization later develops the scoped
roles that methods can perform.

## Representations and Scoped Predictive Models

Representation is the first hard problem because it determines what study
participants can see. Architecture knowledge lives in many forms, including natural-language
specifications, ISA documents, traces, graphs, simulator configurations, RTL,
compiler IR, EDA reports, benchmark metadata, scripts, and review notes. Hidden
defaults, undocumented constraints, and forgotten rejections create state an
automated participant cannot inspect. The result may be invalid, misleading, or
impossible to replay even when the generated artifact looks plausible.

This preview follows the distinction defined in
@sec-data-representations-world-models. The representation says what is encoded;
a scoped consequence model says what the loop predicts or constrains after an
action. A simulator, learned surrogate, calibrated equation, or design rule may
play that broader role, and each has a limited scope and failure mode. The
narrowest case conditions a prediction on represented state and a proposed
action.

QuArch, an architecture-centric benchmark dataset, makes the boundary concrete.
It can assess whether a model knows architecture concepts, but not whether an
automated participant has the represented workload, tool, action, evidence, and challenge state
needed to synthesize or reject a candidate
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. That distinction is the bridge to
@sec-data-representations-world-models.

## Tool Interfaces for AI-Assisted Studies

An Architecture 2.0 environment combines an architecture tool with an
interface that defines how an automated participant may use it. The interface
specifies legal actions, available observations, execution cost, provenance,
and failure behavior. @fig-tools-become-environments contrasts such an
environment with a basic wrapper that merely submits a command and returns a
result.

![**Information required for an executable tool environment.** Legal actions, observations, costs, fidelity, provenance, invalid-action semantics, and failure signals let a reviewer trace tool feedback. The environment does not determine whether that feedback is sufficient evidence or decide the architectural claim.](images/F4e-tools-become-environments){#fig-tools-become-environments width="100%" fig-alt="Side-by-side diagram contrasting a basic tool wrapper with an architecture environment that specifies legal actions, observations, cost and fidelity, provenance, invalid-action semantics, and failure signals."}

ArchGym, an open-source evaluation environment, is an important example because
it treats the connection between search algorithms and architecture simulators as
a first-class interface [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. A simulator wrapper becomes
architecturally useful only when it specifies legal actions,
invalid-action semantics, feedback cost, provenance, comparable baselines, and
failure signals. @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces expands
this point and asks what such environments can and cannot prove.

## Agents and Methods Have Roles in a Compound System

The word "agent" can hide too much. In credible Architecture 2.0 systems, there
may be several roles rather than one monolithic actor. A generator proposes
candidates. A predictor estimates behavior before expensive evaluation. An
optimizer chooses what to try next. A critic challenges assumptions. A repair
role proposes limited corrections to artifacts that fail a check. A verifier
checks constraints. An explanation role documents tradeoffs, failures, and rejected
alternatives. A coordinator decomposes work, routes tool calls, and
tracks state, provenance, and dependencies. An architect or
named engineering team sets intent, declares decision rights, and decides
what evidence is enough for consequential claims.

Language models, search procedures, learned surrogates, scripts, formal tools,
and people can fill those roles in different combinations. Review should ask
what role acts, what state it consumes, what action it takes, and what checks or
challenges its output.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops these compositions.

The architect's role does not depend on a prediction that AI will remain unable
to perform these jobs. Architects use empirical results to choose objectives
and abstractions, weigh cross-layer tradeoffs, judge acceptable risk, and
recommend organizational commitments. AI may perform most of the execution and
may propose those judgments. A named person or engineering organization must
still be responsible for the choices. This does not require a
person to approve every action. It requires stated responsibility for the
study's objectives, exceptions, and consequential decisions.

## Feedback Becomes Evidence

Feedback is not evidence by default. A simulator result, benchmark score,
synthesis report, generated explanation, or model confidence value is feedback.
It becomes evidence only when it is tied to a claim, its scope, an
interpretation, and a provenance trail.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="A 3 W claim needs evidence, not a number"}
**Context.** A reported power result is feedback. It becomes evidence only when
the study records enough state for a reviewer to judge what the number means.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** If a generative method claims that a "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" for the "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
meets the "3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP mobile process," the study must
record the workload, input distribution, memory traffic, power model, process
assumptions, compiled software stack, rejected alternatives, uncertainty, and
the observation or check that would count against the claim.

**Evidence rule.** The same power number has different authority as a proxy
estimate, cycle-level simulation, synthesis result, post-layout estimate, or
silicon measurement. A proxy estimate may support exploration; post-layout or
silicon evidence is needed before stronger implementation or deployment
commitments.

The design-space report must say which evidence level supports the claim and
which commitment boundary it has not crossed.
:::

Evidence also includes negative information. Rejected candidates, failed
simulator runs, invalid configurations, proxy wins that disappear at higher
fidelity, and assumptions that had to be abandoned are architecture data. They
help explain why a surviving candidate deserves
attention and what would overturn the claim. @sec-feedback-verification-trust
develops the evidence, fidelity, and rejection rules this preview only names.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Feedback and Evidence Check"}
Before committing a model-generated candidate, locate the evidence pointer, the
result and check that could invalidate the candidate, the authority that may
stop advancement, and the decision owner. If these are undefined,
the output cannot pass the commitment boundary.
:::

## The Design-Loop Card

> **Design-loop card.** A design-loop card is a compact index into one
> architecture study. It states or points to the study's scope, architectural
> claim, execution process, supporting and contrary evidence, rejection path,
> commitment boundary, and next decision owner. Detailed state, evidence,
> and replay artifacts remain in linked records.

The card is one practical instrument for applying the ontology. It gives a
compact way to inspect a paper, project, benchmark, or internal study while
the full evidence record stays in linked artifacts. A reader may fill it in or
use it diagnostically.

For the lighthouse prompt, a single compact card indexes the entire study
(@fig-design-loop-card-example). It gives a reviewer one place to find the
claim, study boundary, execution path, and records that support a decision. The
underlying artifacts remain in their source files.

![**A design-loop card indexes one architecture study:** It points readers to the study scope, claim, execution process, evidence, and decision owner. Detailed state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="100%" fig-alt="A compact study index card for the lighthouse prompt, listing the twelve canonical card fields from intent and task through evidence, rejection checks, claim boundary, and decision owner, under a claim banner, with a footer noting that the card indexes rather than replaces the study records."}

The card is deliberately simple. Its purpose is to reveal whether a claimed
Architecture 2.0 contribution provides
enough information about its study and claim for review. A paper that reports a better search result
but hides the cost and availability of feedback, rejected candidates, or
environment validity is hard to compare. A tool that produces designs but cannot say what rejects them
is hard to trust. A benchmark that measures model accuracy but not
architecture-relevant reasoning may be useful, but it should not be mistaken
for a complete architecture study.
Missing card fields signal missing disclosure, not automatic failure. Ask for
evidence, escalate fidelity, reject the claim, or narrow the commitment until
the study can support what it reports. A structurally complete card can still
index weak architecture. Completion is not evidence of quality, novelty,
insight, or correctness. Whether card-first review catches missing evidence
more consistently than ordinary review is itself a testable claim; the first
open question below states it as one.

@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full twelve-field card and review
rubric. Detailed evidence and replay packages remain linked artifacts rather
than additional card fields. A replay package records executable inputs, tool
and environment versions, parameters, and outputs for an attempted rerun. A
major Architecture 2.0 claim should provide enough of its study for another
architect to reconstruct and challenge it.

@sec-data-representations-world-models,
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces,
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, and
@sec-feedback-verification-trust examine the information indexed by the card in
greater detail.

## Open Research Questions

The research questions below ask how much information reviewers need, which
workload and baseline records make a claim credible, and whether structured
review can identify problems before expensive execution.

1. **What minimum record lets another architect review AI-assisted work?**
An AI-assisted architecture study should state its question, workload scope,
baseline, design space, represented state, environment, method assignments,
supporting and contrary evidence, challenge process, decision rights, and
commitment boundary. A strong contribution would test how much of that content
can be indexed without requiring the author's full internal toolchain. The
evaluation should measure whether independent reviewers identify missing
evidence, unsupported commitments, and unreviewable transitions more
consistently than they do from a normal paper artifact. The design-loop card in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card provides one starting point.

2. **What workload and baseline records make a claim credible?**
Another architect can review workload characterization when a paper records input
distributions, benchmark construction, excluded cases, holdout workloads,
software versions, baseline assumptions, and provenance. A paper-quality result
would define a workload and baseline packet for AI-assisted architecture claims,
then test whether reviewers can tell where a result generalizes and where it
does not. This question sharpens the ontology by making workload scope part of
the claim rather than background context.

3. **How can holdout benchmarks detect study or method overfitting?**
An AI-assisted study can overfit its methods and conclusions to a benchmark
suite, trace slice, proxy model, or metric available to the reviewer. A strong research
artifact would define holdout construction methods and evaluate whether they
catch design-space lessons that fail on shifted workloads, changed baselines,
or stronger feedback. The result should tell reviewers when a method has learned
an architectural relationship and when it has learned the evaluation surface.

4. **How can rejected alternatives become auditable design-space evidence?**
Rejected candidates help bound the claim because they show which regions of
the design space were considered and why they failed. A research contribution
would define a record for rejected alternatives, invalid actions, failed
timing closures, proxy failures, and repaired candidates, then test whether
reviewers can reconstruct a design-space boundary from that record. This is the
negative-evidence version of the ontology in @sec-arch2-ontology.

5. **What can structured review catch before expensive execution?**
A study record should be useful before tools run, not only after a result
exists. A strong paper would evaluate which disclosed properties predict later
failure modes such as missing baselines, invalid action spaces, unsupported
evidence, or commitment boundaries that are too broad. The research artifact
could be a static checker, review protocol, authoring tool, or design-loop card
that flags under-specified claims while leaving architectural judgment with
named reviewers.

## Conclusion

A well-documented AI-assisted architecture study now has a defined unit of work, a
claim tied to a workload and comparator, assigned decision rights, and an
execution record. An architect can use those elements to scope the authority
granted to an automated participant and read an unfamiliar study in three
passes of scope, execution, and judgment.

The framework keeps five elements distinct:

- **Bounded study:** The unit of work.
- **Possible outcomes:** Understanding, artifacts, findings, and decisions.
- **Architectural claim:** The unit of review, stating an outcome with its
  workload, baseline, design space, evidence, counterevidence, and claim
  boundary.
- **Design loop:** The execution and analysis process used when the study's
  work iterates.
- **Supporting instruments:** The ontology, card, evidence record, checks, and
  replay package that support review at different depths.

Reviewability and replay remain separate. Even a successful replay of a
replay package does not establish that the architectural
claim, baseline, or decision was adequate.

None of those instruments guarantees correctness or architectural value. A
complete record can support a weak claim, and a replayable experiment can answer
the wrong question. These instruments help another architect determine what
information the study used, what it measured, how the result was challenged,
and who made the decision. Those questions will remain relevant even as models
and tools change because they arise from ordinary architecture practice.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Keep the three concepts distinct. The bounded study defines the work, the architectural claim states what another architect must evaluate, and the design loop records how the iterative work proceeded.
- **Reader test:** Can you identify the architecture question, AI role,
  alternatives, tool-connected experiments, claim, counterevidence,
  mechanism-based explanation, and scoped decision?
- **Up next:** @sec-data-representations-world-models asks what representations and scoped predictive models must encode before AI methods can act on architecture state credibly.
:::
